10.text: This lab is mostly about counting and merging, but we still need a little math later to measure whether the learned subwords actually help the language model.

11.text: BPE learns entirely from the corpus itself, so the first step is simply to find the raw names file we will mine for repeated character patterns.

12.text: The merge history depends on corpus statistics, so we keep the setup stable enough that the same subwords emerge from run to run.

14.text: BPE is a cumulative greedy process: early merges shape every later token, so reproducibility matters more than it first appears.

19.text: Locate the shared names corpus. Every later merge rule will be discovered from these strings alone, with no hand-written vocabulary.

20.text: Fetch the corpus if needed so the tokenizer can be learned from scratch with no manual setup.

21.text: Only import the downloader if the file is missing. The real story starts once we begin counting recurring fragments.

23.text: A tiny but realistic corpus: enough repeated fragments for subwords like an, er, and ia to emerge naturally.

24.text: Fetch the file from the internet once and save it locally. After this first download, every future run loads instantly from disk.

26.text: Load every name, strip whitespace, skip blanks. Each name becomes one "document" for training.

27.text: Shuffle so the model doesn't memorize alphabetical order.

28.text: A quick sanity check that we loaded the full dataset. With ~32K names, we have plenty of data to discover meaningful merge patterns.

34.text: Collect every unique character (a-z), this becomes the base vocabulary, same as lab 01.

35.text: Character-to-integer mapping. In labs 01-08, this WAS the tokenizer. Now it's the baseline we'll beat.

36.text: Reverse mapping for decoding back to text.

37.text: BOS (beginning/end of sequence) gets the next available id, just past the last character.

38.text: 27 tokens total: 26 lowercase letters plus one special BOS token. This tiny vocabulary is the starting point for both tokenizers.

46.text: Encode a name to character tokens: wrap with BOS on both ends, look up each character's integer ID. This is the baseline that BPE will improve upon.

48.text: BOS + character ids + BOS. Identical to how labs 01-08 encode, just factored into a reusable function.

51.text: Reverse the encoding: map each token ID back to its character, skipping the BOS markers.

53.text: Skip BOS tokens, map ids back to characters, join into a string.

71.text: 50 merges is tiny, GPT-2 uses 50,000. But even 50 will noticeably compress our names corpus.

76.text: Reuse the same BOS id so char and BPE tokenizers are comparable.

77.text: 27 base tokens before any merges happen.

81.text: The corpus starts as character-level token sequences, identical to what the char tokenizer produces.

82.text: Build the initial corpus for BPE training: each name becomes a list of integer token IDs, starting at character granularity.

83.text: Wrap with BOS, convert characters to integer ids. This is the starting point BPE will compress.

84.text: Accumulate each tokenized name into the corpus list. This gives us the complete training set that BPE will iteratively compress through merges.

87.text: The heart of BPE: count how often each adjacent pair of tokens appears across the entire corpus.

89.text: Simple dictionary to accumulate pair frequencies.

90.text: Scan every tokenized name in the corpus.

91.text: Slide a window of size 2 across each sequence.

92.text: Each pair is a tuple of two adjacent token ids.

93.text: Increment the count. The pair with the highest count will be merged next.

97.text: The core merge operation: scan every sequence left-to-right, replacing adjacent tokens matching the pair with the new merged ID. This is greedy — the leftmost match wins.

99.text: Allocate a fresh corpus list that will hold the post-merge versions of every sequence. We rebuild rather than mutate in place to keep the logic clean.

101.text: Walk through the old sequence building a new one token by token, replacing any occurrence of the target pair with the merged token along the way.

102.text: Manual index, we sometimes skip ahead by 2 when we find the pair.

103.text: Walk through the sequence looking for the target pair.

104.text: Pattern matching: if the current token and the next match our target pair, emit the merged token and skip ahead by two positions.

105.text: Replace the pair with the single merged token, this is where compression happens.

106.text: Skip past both original tokens since we consumed them as one.

108.text: No match here, keep the original token.

109.text: Move the scan pointer forward by one position. We only skip by two when we find and merge the target pair; otherwise we advance normally.

110.text: This name is done, add its merged sequence to the new corpus.

117.text: The merge table: maps each learned pair to its new token id. This IS the tokenizer.

118.text: Tracks the growing vocabulary size as merges create new tokens.

121.text: Recursive decoding: a merged token is decoded by looking up which two tokens were merged to create it, then decoding each of those. Base case: character tokens decode directly.

123.text: BOS decodes to empty string, it's a control token, not a character.

125.text: Base characters decode directly via the character mapping.

128.text: For merged tokens, find which pair was merged and recursively decode both halves.

129.text: Linear scan through merges, fine for 50 merges, would need a reverse map at scale.

130.text: Recursive call: a merged token might itself be composed of merged tokens.

134.text: Human-friendly token display for the training log.

136.text: Show BOS as a readable tag.

140.text: Merged tokens show their full decoded string (e.g., token 28 might display as "an").

148.text: The main BPE training loop: 50 rounds of find-best-pair, merge, repeat.

149.text: Count all adjacent pairs in the current corpus.

150.text: Safety check, if every sequence is length 1, nothing left to merge.

154.text: Greedily pick the most frequent pair. This greedy choice is what makes BPE BPE.

155.text: Show the starting vocabulary size before any merges are applied.

156.text: Announce how many merge operations we will perform.

157.text: Record this merge in the table. During encoding, merges are replayed in this exact order.

159.text: Log the first 20 merges and every 10th after, watch common English patterns emerge ("an", "er", "in").

160.text: The BPE training loop: each iteration finds the most frequent pair, creates a new token for it, and merges it everywhere in the corpus. Greedy, local, and surprisingly effective.

163.text: Apply the merge to the entire corpus, every occurrence of the pair becomes one token.

164.text: Vocabulary grows by one with each merge.

166.text: Greedy selection: always merge the globally most frequent pair. Not optimal, but close — and fast.

169.text: Record this merge in the table. The order matters: during encoding, merges are replayed in exactly this sequence.

171.text: Log the first 20 merges and every 10th after that, plus the very last one — so the output stays readable.

177.text: Encoding with BPE: start from characters, then replay all merges in order. This is how GPT-2 encodes text.

180.text: Start exactly like character encoding, BOS + char ids + BOS.

182.text: Replay merges in training order. Earlier merges are higher priority (more frequent patterns).

183.text: Apply each merge to the token sequence. After all merges, common subwords are single tokens.

193.text: A mix of short, long, common, and unusual names to show compression varies.

195.text: Apply each merge by calling merge_pair on a single-element list, then unwrap. The sequence gets shorter with each applicable merge.

197.text: Remove duplicates while preserving order, cap at 10.

201.text: For each example, encode with both tokenizers and show the compression ratio.

202.text: Character encoding: always len(name) + 2 tokens (one per char plus two BOS markers).

203.text: BPE encoding: fewer tokens because common patterns like "an", "er" are now single tokens.

204.text: Compression ratio, how many character tokens each BPE token replaces on average.

205.text: A mix of hand-picked and random names to show how BPE compression varies: short common names compress heavily, long unusual ones less so.

216.text: Sum up character tokens across the entire corpus.

217.text: Sum up BPE tokens across the entire corpus, this is the real compression measurement.

218.text: Average tokens per name with character encoding.

219.text: Average tokens per name with BPE, expect roughly 1.3-1.5x shorter.

228.text: The headline number: BPE compresses the corpus by ~1.3-1.5x with just 50 merges.

241.text: Display the full merge table, this IS the BPE vocabulary, showing exactly what patterns the algorithm discovered.

242.text: Decode each merged token to see its string representation.

243.text: Show the components that were merged (which might themselves be merged tokens).

262.text: A bigram model is the simplest possible language model: just a transition probability table. No neural network needed.

265.text: 2D count matrix: counts[a][b] = how often token b follows token a.

268.text: Tally one more occurrence of this token-to-token transition. The row with the highest total count will later define the most probable next tokens.

272.text: Add-1 (Laplace) smoothing prevents zero probabilities for unseen pairs.

273.text: Normalize to a proper probability distribution.

277.text: Generate a name by sampling one token at a time from the bigram distribution.

279.text: Start with BOS, the model will sample what typically follows the start marker.

282.text: Look up the probability distribution for the current (last) token.

283.text: Sample from the cumulative distribution using a uniform random number.

285.text: Walk through tokens by probability until we pass the random threshold.

287.text: This token was selected, the cumulative probability exceeded our random draw.

290.text: Ancestral sampling from the bigram: pick a random next token weighted by the transition probabilities, repeat until BOS (end) or max length.

295.text: Average log-likelihood per token measures how well the model predicts the training data.

297.text: Accumulate total log-likelihood across all tokens.

300.text: For each adjacent pair in each sequence, look up its probability.

301.text: The probability assigned to this actual transition, higher is better.

303.text: Log probability, the fundamental unit of information theory. Sums are easier than products.

305.text: Average over all tokens gives a per-token measure, comparable across different vocab sizes.

310.text: Encode the entire corpus with the character tokenizer for bigram training.

311.text: Train the character-level bigram, just counting transitions in a 27x27 table.

312.text: Measure how well it models the data. Lower perplexity = better model.

319.text: Encode the entire corpus with BPE, shorter sequences but larger vocabulary.

320.text: Train the BPE bigram, a 77x77 transition table. Each step now covers more text.

321.text: The BPE bigram should have better perplexity per token because each token carries more information.

334.text: Evaluate the BPE bigram on the BPE-encoded corpus.

335.text: Sample a complete name by repeatedly drawing from the transition table.

336.text: Decode character ids back to a readable string.

339.text: Generate from the BPE bigram, each step jumps through common letter patterns, so names sound a bit more natural.

341.text: Sample from the BPE bigram's 77x77 transition table.

342.text: Decode BPE tokens back to a string, merged tokens expand to multiple characters.

175.text: Apply the merge to every sequence in the corpus. The corpus shrinks because two tokens become one wherever this pair appeared.

189.text: Encoding with learned merges: start from characters, then replay every merge in training order. This greedy left-to-right strategy is how GPT-2/3/4 encode text.

194.text: Apply merges in the order they were learned. Earlier merges captured the most frequent patterns, so they fire most often.

229.text: Corpus-wide statistics: total tokens before and after BPE. The compression ratio shows how much shorter sequences become — directly translating to faster training and inference.

254.text: Display every learned merge rule. Reading this table reveals what patterns the algorithm discovered: common bigrams first (like 'an', 'er'), then longer chunks.

275.text: A bigram model is the simplest possible language model: just a table of P(next_token | current_token). No neural network — pure counting plus Laplace smoothing to handle unseen pairs.

308.text: Evaluate model quality: average log-probability the model assigns to the actual next token. Higher means the model predicts the data better.

323.text: Train the character-level bigram on the full corpus. With only 27 tokens, each bigram step covers one character — short-range patterns only.

332.text: Train the BPE bigram on the same corpus. With 227 tokens, each step covers a subword — so a single bigram transition can capture multi-character patterns like 'an' → 'na'.

347.text: Generate names from the character bigram. Quality is limited because each step only sees one character of context.

352.text: Generate names from the BPE bigram. Even with the same simple bigram model, BPE tokens capture longer patterns, producing more plausible names.

364.text: The punchline: BPE is the algorithm behind every modern LLM tokenizer. At GPT scale (50K-100K merges on internet text), it's essential to keep sequences manageable.

1.text: Module docstring: this lab implements Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch, then compares character-level and BPE tokenization with a simple bigram model.

33.text: Every unique character gets an integer id. BOS marks the start and end of each sequence — the model learns when to stop generating.

40.text: Print a section header. The character tokenizer is the baseline that BPE will be measured against.

43.text: Show the vocabulary breakdown: how many real characters vs control tokens.

47.text: Docstring for the character encoder function.

52.text: Docstring for the character decoder function.

59.text: BPE starts with the character vocabulary and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a new token.

60.text: This is exactly how GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, and LLaMA build their tokenizers — at much larger scale with ~50K merges.

63.text: The fundamental tradeoff: larger vocab means shorter sequences (faster) but more embedding parameters.

67.text: Print a section header for the BPE tokenizer training phase.

70.text: Section separator before the BPE hyperparameter.

72.text: 200 merge operations is tiny compared to production (GPT-2 uses 50K). But it's enough to see clear compression on a names corpus.

73.text: Step 1 comment: start with character-level tokens for each name, then iteratively merge.

78.text: Base vocabulary size includes all characters plus the BOS marker. Every merge will add one more token on top of this.

80.text: Comment: build the initial corpus by wrapping each name with BOS and converting to integer IDs.

85.text: End of the corpus building loop. Every name is now a list of integers ready for the merge iterations.

88.text: Docstring: count how often each adjacent token pair appears across the entire corpus.

94.text: Return the complete frequency dictionary. The pair with the highest count will be merged next.

98.text: Docstring for merge_pair: replace every occurrence of a given pair with a new single token ID.

107.text: Else branch: no pair match at this position. Fall through to copy the current token unchanged.

111.text: Return the fully merged corpus. Every sequence is now shorter by the number of merges that fired.

114.text: Step 2 comment: iteratively merge the most frequent pair. Each merge creates a new token and shrinks all sequences.

116.text: The merge table records what was merged — it IS the tokenizer, replayed during encoding.

119.text: Blank line separating the merge table setup from the decode function.

122.text: Docstring: decode a single BPE token ID to its string representation, recursively expanding merges.

124.text: BOS decodes to empty string. It's a control token with no visible text representation.

126.text: Base characters (a-z) decode directly from the id-to-character lookup table.

131.text: Fallback for unknown token IDs — should never happen in practice.

135.text: Docstring: like bpe_decode_token but keeps BOS visible as '|' for human-readable output.

141.text: For merged tokens, recursively expand both components into their display form.

142.text: Concatenate the display strings of both halves of the merge.

146.text: Helper function for the training log: convert any token ID to a human-readable string.

151.text: Base characters display as themselves — single letter output.

152.text: Merged tokens use bpe_decode_token to recursively expand to their full string.

158.text: Print dashes to separate the table header from the data rows.

161.text: Count all adjacent pairs in the current (possibly already-merged) corpus.

162.text: Safety check: if no pairs remain (all sequences are length 0 or 1), stop early.

167.text: Record the frequency of the best pair for the training log.

168.text: Assign a new token ID: simply the current vocabulary size, so IDs increment from 27 upward.

172.text: Build a human-readable string showing which tokens were merged (e.g., 'a'+'n').

173.text: Print one row of the training log: merge number, pair IDs, visual form, frequency, and new vocab size.

176.text: Increment the vocabulary size. Every merge adds exactly one new token.

178.text: Print the final BPE vocabulary size: base tokens plus all learned merges.

181.text: Full-sequence decoder: given a list of BPE token IDs, reconstruct the original string.

192.text: Start with character-level tokens, exactly like the character encoder. BPE encoding then compresses from here.

196.text: Return the final compressed token sequence. Common subwords are now single tokens.

207.text: Add some random names from the dataset so the examples are not cherry-picked.

209.text: Deduplicate while preserving order and cap at 10 examples.

214.text: Loop through each example name, encoding it with both tokenizers for comparison.

215.text: Encode with the character tokenizer: always len(name) + 2 tokens (one per char plus two BOS).

230.text: Encode every name with BPE and sum up all tokens across the corpus.

231.text: Average character tokens per name — the baseline measurement.

232.text: Average BPE tokens per name — should be notably less than the character average.

234.text: Print the total number of names in the dataset.

237.text: Print the total character tokens across the corpus — the 'before' number.

238.text: Print the total BPE tokens across the corpus — the 'after' number.

249.text: Print the base tokens (a-z) that form the foundation of the vocabulary.

250.text: Show which token ID is assigned to the BOS marker.

255.text: Decode the merged token to show what string it represents (e.g., token 28 = 'an').

256.text: Decode the first component of the merge, replacing the pipe symbol with a readable BOS tag.

258.text: Print one row: the new token ID, its decoded string, and which two tokens were merged to create it.

263.text: Comment: the bigram model is P(next_token | current_token), just a lookup table of transition counts.

276.text: Docstring: train a bigram model by counting transitions, smoothing, and normalizing.

278.text: Initialize a 2D count matrix of zeros: rows are current tokens, columns are next tokens.

281.text: Increment the count for this specific token-to-token transition.

284.text: Process each row of counts to produce a row of probabilities.

286.text: Laplace smoothing: add 1 to each count, then normalize. This ensures no probability is exactly zero.

291.text: Docstring: sample a sequence from the bigram model by repeatedly drawing next tokens.

292.text: Start the generated sequence with BOS — the model will sample what typically follows the start marker.

296.text: Draw a uniform random number to use for sampling from the probability distribution.

298.text: Walk through tokens by probability until the cumulative sum exceeds the random threshold.

304.text: Return the generated sequence once it terminates.

309.text: Docstring: compute average log-likelihood per token to measure model quality.

313.text: For each adjacent pair in each sequence, look up the probability the model assigns to that transition.

316.text: Add the log of the transition probability. Log-space addition avoids floating-point underflow from multiplying many small numbers.

318.text: Return the average: total log-likelihood divided by number of tokens. This per-token measure is comparable across different vocab sizes.

324.text: Train the character bigram: count transitions in a 27x27 table, smooth, and normalize.

325.text: Evaluate the character bigram: compute average log-likelihood on the training data.

327.text: Print the average log-likelihood per token for the character bigram.

328.text: Perplexity: exp(-avg_ll). Lower is better. Intuitively, it's the effective number of equally-likely choices at each step.

333.text: Train the BPE bigram: count transitions in a vocab_size x vocab_size table.

337.text: Print the BPE perplexity. Expect lower perplexity per token since each BPE token carries more information.

348.text: Sample a complete name from the character bigram by drawing from the 27x27 transition table.

349.text: Decode the character token IDs back to a readable string.

353.text: Sample a complete name from the BPE bigram. Each step can jump through multi-character patterns.

354.text: Sample from the BPE bigram's larger transition table.

355.text: Decode BPE tokens back to a string — merged tokens expand to multiple characters automatically.

365.text: Print the compression summary: how many tokens before vs after BPE, and the compression ratio.

369.text: This is the algorithm behind GPT-2, LLaMA, and most modern LLM tokenizers. At GPT scale, BPE with 50K-100K merges is essential.
